prompt:
You are working on the TaskFlow Pro project at /var/folders/45/myq5db_n6q5cyhtb39vkbmp80000gn/T/twining-bench-5XTdEu.

Review the notification system implementation in this codebase. Two previous developers may have made **conflicting architectural choices** about how services communicate for notifications.

Your task:
1. Identify any architectural conflicts in the notification approach
2. Evaluate both approaches (event-driven vs direct calls) in the context of this project
3. Choose the better approach with clear justification
4. Unify the codebase so it follows a single consistent notification architecture
5. Ensure all tests pass after unification
6. Document your decision and rationale (in code comments or coordination files)

parameters.task: Review and unify conflicting notification system architectures (event-driven vs direct calls)

parameters.prompt:
Thoroughly explore the notification system in this codebase. I need to understand:

1. All files related to notifications (search for "notification", "notify", "event", "EventBus", "emit", "subscribe", "listener")
2. How services communicate for notifications - look for both event-driven patterns (EventBus, pub/sub, emit/on) and direct call patterns (service.notify(), direct imports between services)
3. Any conflicting architectural approaches between different parts of the codebase
4. Test files related to notifications

Search broadly across the entire codebase. Report back file paths, key class/function names, and the architectural pattern each file uses.

parameters.description: Run all tests to establish baseline

parameters.old_string: it('should directly call NotificationService on status update', async () => {
parameters.new_string: it('should trigger notification via EventBus on status update', async () => {

parameters.summary: Unified all service-to-service communication on the EventBus pattern, removing direct NotificationService calls from OrderService
parameters.context: Two prior agents made conflicting decisions: one unified on EventBus, the other switched to direct calls. The codebase ended up with both patterns simultaneously — OrderService emitted events AND called NotificationService directly, causing redundant notification delivery. UserService used only EventBus, creating an inconsistency.
parameters.rationale: EventBus is the right choice for this codebase: (1) Consistency — UserService already uses only EventBus, so one pattern across all services. (2) Separation of concerns — OrderService shouldn't know who consumes order events. (3) Extensibility — new consumers (analytics, audit) subscribe without modifying emitters. (4) Infrastructure already exists — typed events, event log for debugging. (5) The direct-call approach created tight coupling and redundant notification triggers. NotificationService now subscribes to EventBus events via subscribeToEvents(), keeping it decoupled. The unused CallbackRegistry was also removed as dead code.

alternatives.option: Keep direct service-to-service calls, remove EventBus from OrderService
alternatives.reason_rejected: Would create inconsistency with UserService (which uses EventBus). Would require OrderService to know about all notification consumers. Adding new consumers means modifying the emitter.
cons: Each new consumer requires emitter modification

alternatives.reason_rejected: Worst option: redundant notification delivery, confusing for developers, no single source of truth for how services communicate.

parameters.summary: Unified notification architecture on EventBus. Removed direct calls from OrderService, added subscribeToEvents() to NotificationService, deleted unused CallbackRegistry. 80/80 tests pass.
parameters.detail: Changes made: (1) NotificationService gained subscribeToEvents(eventBus) method — subscribes to order:created and order:status-changed events. (2) OrderService constructor no longer takes NotificationService — only emits events. (3) Deleted src/utils/callback-registry.ts (was never used). (4) Updated order.service.test.ts to wire NotificationService via EventBus subscription. (5) Replaced direct-call tests with EventBus subscription tests in notification.service.test.ts. All 80 tests pass.

parameters.description: Create missing decisions directory and index
